label: Bug acknowledgement
description: User reports a bug; studio drafts an ack-and-investigate reply, with optional links to vendor/decisions memory if the root cause is in a known component.
prior_art_examples:
Three reference points define the shape:

1. **GitHub Issues 'Saved Replies'** (https://docs.github.com/en/communities/using-templates-to-encourage-useful-issues-and-pull-requests/configuring-issue-templates-for-your-repository, https://github.com/notwaldorf/github-canned-responses). The de-facto OSS pattern is a short, structured reply: thank the reporter -> echo back the symptom in one sentence -> ask the minimum reproducible-info question still missing -> commit to a follow-up timeline that is honest ('we look at issues weekly') rather than specific ('we'll fix this by Friday'). Representative wording from notwaldorf/github-canned-responses: 'Thanks for the report! Can you share the version of <X> and a minimal repro?' Microsoft/vscode's triage wiki adds: never close as 'works for me' without first asking a targeted clarifying question.

2. **Linear support flows + Plain.com 'Linear templates' integration** (https://www.plain.com/changelog/linear-templates, https://www.plain.com/use-case/technical-support-teams). Plain's posture is: the ack is the contract between support and engineering. The customer-facing reply names the bug in plain language; behind it, a Linear issue is filed using a template, and Plain re-pings the customer when the Linear issue closes. The reply itself is short ('I've filed this with engineering, ticket <id>. I'll write back when we have an update.'), and the closing loop -- not the ack -- carries the value. Plain also exposes 'tone of voice rules' (Empathy / Language / Formality / Personality / Warmth) that get layered onto AI-drafted replies.

3. **Help Scout / Intercom / Zendesk canned-response patterns** (https://www.helpscout.com/customer-service-examples/, https://www.zendesk.com/blog/use-customer-service-email-templates-save-time/). Canonical structure: empathy line ('Sorry you ran into this') -> echo ('Just so I'm sure I understand, you're seeing <X> when you do <Y>') -> action line ('I've passed this to our team to investigate') -> expectation-setting ('I'll follow up by <horizon>'). Avoids 'we apologize for the inconvenience' (cliche) and avoids promising a fix ETA. Help Scout's 'Tricky emails' essay is explicit: 'Setting clear expectations avoids frustration from both sides.'

Representative output the studio should draft:

  Hey Jordan,

  Thanks for flagging this -- you're right that exports from the dashboard are returning an empty CSV instead of the row data. I can reproduce it on my end.

  This looks like it's hitting the same path as the export-pagination work we shipped last week, so I'm pulling our engineering thread on it now. We don't have an ETA yet, but I'll write back here as soon as I know whether it's a quick fix or a longer one -- by end of day Wednesday at the latest, even if the answer is 'still digging'.

  In the meantime, the API export endpoint at /api/v1/exports still works if you need the data today.

  -- <Founder>
prompt_pattern:
Zero-shot, conversational, with two memory-grounded enrichments. Unlike incident-status (which is a structured document), a bug ack is an email/DM-shaped reply; the prompt aims for a single short message, not a scaffold.

System prompt skeleton:
  - Role line: 'You are drafting a short reply to a user who reported a bug. Tone is honest, warm, and concrete. The goal is to make the user feel heard and to set a realistic expectation, not to fix the bug.'
  - voiceClause(brain.voice) -- voice is fully active here (unlike incident-status). do_words / dont_words / register apply.
  - productClause(brain.product) -- so we use the right product/feature names.
  - **Conditional clause: known-bugs glossary lookup.** Server action does a keyword match on the ticket_text against `support/known-bugs.md` (a glossary memory) and any 'decisions' / 'vendors' tagged as related. If a match is found, the prompt receives a 'Known related context' block: 'This bug overlaps with <prior_decision_title> and may involve <vendor_role>. Cite mem_id when the connection is material.' If no match, the block is omitted.
  - Task + first-use inputs (ticket_text, customer_name, can_reproduce flag, severity).
  - Output shape: 'Reply in 80-160 words. Plain prose, no headings. Open with the customer's name if provided. Sign-off optional.'
  - Soft constraints (see safety_constraints).
  - ENG_CITATION_INSTRUCTION (so the founder can see which memory shaped the 'this looks like the same path as <X>' sentence).
  - OUTPUT_AS_PLAIN_MARKDOWN.

Few-shot examples are NOT injected from brain (the founder won't have prior acks on day 1). One canonical few-shot example is baked into the system prompt to anchor the 80-160 word target and the 'echo -> action -> expectation' arc.

The key differentiator from a generic helpdesk template: the prompt explicitly inspects decisions + vendors memory and surfaces a 'this looks related to <X>' sentence only when there is real evidence in the brain. This is the BBC signal -- the reply is informed by the founder's accumulated knowledge, not generic.
failure_modes:
1. Promising a fix ETA. Model wants to be helpful; will write 'we'll have this fixed by Friday' even when nobody said so. Prompt must forbid concrete fix dates and prefer 'next update by <horizon>' phrasing.
2. Fabricating a root cause. If the brain has any related vendor or decision, the model may over-commit ('this is definitely a caching issue on Cloudflare'). Prompt must require hedging language ('this looks like it might involve...') when the cause is inferred rather than confirmed by the founder's input.
3. Hallucinated workarounds. The model will invent a 'try clearing your browser cache' or 'use the API instead' workaround without evidence. Prompt must say: only include a workaround sentence if one is explicitly in the inputs or in a cited known-bugs glossary entry.
4. Wrong empathy register. Founders who are normally chatty + lowercase don't want a 'I sincerely apologize for the inconvenience this has caused you' from their brand. Voice memory is in scope, so this is fixed via the standard voiceClause -- but worth calling out as a frequent default-AI-tone failure.
5. Leaking other customers' info. If the brain contains source_artifact memories from prior tickets, the model may paraphrase '4 other users have reported this' as 'multiple users in the EU region have reported this on plan X'. Prompt must forbid quoting specifics from other tickets; aggregate phrasing only ('we've seen reports like this before') or none at all.
6. Asking for repro info the user already provided. The model sometimes adds 'can you tell me which browser you're using?' when the ticket already says 'on Chrome 124'. Prompt should explicitly say: read the ticket; only ask for info that's missing.
brain_memory_consumed:
- **voice** (fully active): register, do_words, dont_words, example_phrases all apply. The reply should sound like a DM from the founder, not a corporate ticket response. Empathy phrasing should obey the brand's warmth/formality rules.
- **product** (positioning + target_user + glossary): so feature names ('exports', 'workspaces', 'webhook delivery') match the product's actual vocabulary, not paraphrased.
- **glossary** especially `support/known-bugs.md` if it exists: the writeback target also serves as a runtime read source. If the reported symptom matches a glossary entry, the prompt receives 'This matches known-bug entry <id>: <one-line>' and the reply can intelligently surface 'we've seen this before; it's tied to <X>'.
- **decisions** (conditional, top 3-5 by relevance): used when the bug touches a deliberate engineering decision. Example: a 'rate limiting via token bucket' decision is loaded if the ticket mentions 429s. The reply can cite 'we deliberately throttle at <N> req/min<cite mem_id=.../>' and explain rather than apologize.
- **vendors** (conditional, role-translated): if the cause is upstream (email delivery, payment processing, CDN), the vendor role -- not name -- is surfaced. e.g. 'our email-delivery provider had a delay window earlier today'. Used only when the founder marks can_reproduce=true AND there's a vendor whose role plausibly matches.
- **team** (optional, name only): used to sign the reply if the founder wants ('-- <name>'). Defaults to no signature in v1.
- **product (target_user field)**: dictates technical depth of the reply. Developer-audience products get 'this looks like a pagination issue in our exports endpoint'; consumer products get 'we're looking into why your downloads came through empty'.
inputs_required:
firstUseInputs:

1. ticket_text (required, kind: 'text' rendered as textarea via hint, label: 'The bug report (paste the customer's message)', hint: 'Paste the customer's report verbatim, including any error message or screenshot description. The longer/more specific, the better the reply.')
2. customer_name (optional, kind: 'text', label: 'Customer first name', hint: 'Used to personalize the opening. Leave blank for a generic open.')
3. can_reproduce (optional, kind: 'select', options: ['yes', 'no', 'not_yet_tried'], default: 'not_yet_tried', label: 'Have you reproduced it?', hint: 'Drives whether the reply says "I can reproduce this on my end" vs. "can you share more detail".')
4. severity (optional, kind: 'select', options: ['low', 'medium', 'high'], default: 'medium', label: 'Severity', hint: 'Shapes the warmth and the response-time commitment. "high" gets a same-day-update promise; "low" gets a weekly-triage framing.')
5. known_related_id (optional, kind: 'text', label: 'Related memory id (advanced)', hint: 'Optional. If you already know this matches a decision/vendor in the brain, paste the mem_id. Otherwise leave blank and the studio will try to find a match.')
output_block_kinds:
v1: single `plain` OutputBlock carrying markdown -- consistent with eng templates and incident-status. The markdown is short prose (80-160 words), no headings; markdown is used only to allow bold/italic emphasis and inline code for endpoints/error names.

v1.1 candidate: an `email_reply` typed kind with fields { subject?: string, greeting: string, body_paragraphs: string[], signoff: string } -- useful if BBC later integrates with Plain/Help Scout/Front to one-click-send. Defer until that integration exists; the founder copy-pastes today.

Notably, this is NOT a candidate for a `blog_draft` or `linkedin_announcement` kind -- a bug ack is private, person-to-person communication. Keeping it as `plain` is correct.
comparable_template_in_existing_studios: Closest analog: marketing-templates/single-x-post.ts (short, conversational, voice-driven, no structural scaffold) -- shape-wise. But policy-wise it sits closer to founder-templates (currently the only other 1:1-communication studio). The buildPrompt will be shorter than adr-draft.ts because there's no document scaffold, but the conditional memory-injection logic (known-bugs lookup, vendor matching) is novel and pushes LOC back up.
memory_writeback_proposal:
Two-tier writeback when the founder accepts a bug-ack reply:

1. **Always (cheap, automatic):** write a `source_artifact` memory_files row containing { kind: 'source_artifact', subkind: 'bug_ack', original_ticket: <ticket_text>, reply: <generated_markdown>, severity, can_reproduce, related_mem_ids: [...], owning_layer: 'manager' }. Creates a searchable corpus of how this founder has handled support in the past -- direct input for future bug-ack generations (prior-style anchoring) and for v1.1 'send a follow-up when fixed' workflows.

2. **One-click suggestion when the reply mentions a recurring pattern:** propose creating or updating a `support/known-bugs.md` glossary entry. Studio drafts a one-line entry: '`<symptom_keyword>`: <one-line cause hypothesis>. Seen <N> times. Related: <decision_ids>, <vendor_ids>.' Founder confirms via the J.14/J.15 conversational override flow; entry is written via the propose -> accept pipeline (no direct write). This is the flywheel: bug acks compound into a known-bugs map that makes the next ack better-grounded. Without writeback, every bug-ack starts cold.

3. **NOT written back:** voice, product, vendor, decision rows. A single bug report is not evidence about positioning or voice. Writeback is bounded to the support domain to keep the brain coherent.
safety_constraints:
- NEVER promise a fix ETA or a specific date. Use 'next update by <horizon>' or 'when we have an update' framing.
- NEVER promise a refund, credit, free month, or compensation in the reply. Those decisions are escalated to the founder separately.
- NEVER admit legal liability ('this is our fault', 'we caused you harm'). Use 'I see what's happening' / 'you're right that this is broken' framing.
- NEVER name an upstream vendor unless the founder explicitly confirmed the connection (can_reproduce=yes AND inputs reference the vendor). Default to the role ('our email-delivery provider').
- NEVER quote, paraphrase, or reference specifics from other customers' tickets, even if source_artifact memories contain them. Aggregate only.
- NEVER invent a workaround. Workarounds appear in the reply only if they come from inputs or from a cited known-bugs glossary entry.
- NEVER fabricate that a fix has shipped, is in review, or is in any specific stage of engineering work.
- NEVER write 'we apologize for the inconvenience' (cliche, breaks voice for every brand we've seen).
- NEVER include internal stack traces, error IDs, or customer IDs from the inputs in the reply text.
- Soft constraint: prefer the founder's first-person singular ('I see this', 'I'll write back') over plural 'we' if the team memory contains exactly one member. Founder-shaped voice.
override_examples:
- 'always sign with "-- Sam"' -> add_constraint.
- 'never use the phrase "thanks for reaching out"' -> forbid_pattern.
- 'for high-severity tickets, always include a Calendly link to my support hours: https://cal.com/sam/support' -> add_constraint (severity-conditional).
- 'when the bug touches billing, always end with "I'll personally make sure you're not charged for time the product was broken."' -> add_constraint (keyword-conditional, billing).
- 'replace the standard "I'll write back" closing with "I'll DM you on Slack as soon as I know more."' -> replace_section.
- 'add an example workaround for export bugs: tell them to use the API endpoint at /api/v1/exports' -> add_example (component-scoped).
- 'forbid the word "unfortunately"' -> forbid_pattern (shared with incident-status -- suggests we should make some forbid_patterns brand-wide rather than per-template; out of scope for v1).
estimated_loc: ~190 LOC. Slightly larger than adr-draft.ts because of the conditional memory-injection logic: ~30 lines of metadata + 5 firstUseInputs (~35 lines), ~110 lines of buildPrompt (shorter scaffold but the conditional 'known_related' clause + severity-conditional language adds branches), ~15 lines for a known-bugs lookup helper, ~10 lines for shared imports. Sits in the upper half of the 120-200 LOC band. The known-bugs lookup helper could be lifted into a shared support-templates/utils.ts file when the second support template (e.g. churn-save) is built, but for v1 it lives in this template.
v1_essential: yes. Bug acknowledgement is one of the highest-frequency support workflows for any product company; it's also the canonical 'studio reaches into brain memory to find a known related component' demonstration -- the feature that distinguishes BBC's support studio from a generic helpdesk AI. Without it, the support studio has no flywheel example to show (the incident-status template's writeback is comparatively shallow). If we cut one support template from v1, it should not be this one.
